sentences.base_language: The guest arrived at the hotel and went to the front desk.
sentences.target_language: وصل الضيف إلى الفندق وذهب إلى مكتب الاستقبال.
sentences.romanized: wasala ad-dayf ila al-funduq wa-dhahaba ila maktab al-istiqbal.
sentences.literal: Arrived the-guest to the-hotel and-went to desk the-reception.
sentences.grammarNote: وَصَلَ الضَّيفُ (wasala ad-ḍayf) — the verb comes before the subject in VSO (verb-subject-object) order, very common in Arabic narrative. مَكتَب الاِستِقبَال (maktab al-istiqbāl) = front desk — an iḍāfa chain: مَكتَب (desk/office, from root ك-ت-ب) + الاِستِقبَال (the reception, from Form X of ق-ب-ل).

sentences.base_language: Good evening. I have a reservation under the name Karim.
sentences.target_language: مساء الخير. لدي حجز باسم كريم.
sentences.romanized: masa' al-khayr. ladayya hajz bi-ism Karim.
sentences.literal: Evening the-good. I-have reservation by-name Karim.
sentences.grammarNote: لدي (ladayya) = 'I have,' literally 'at my side.' It is formed from لدى (at/near) + the first-person pronoun suffix ي (i). This is one of several ways to express possession in Arabic: لدي كتاب (I have a book). Compare with عندي (indi) which is more colloquial but means the same.

sentences.base_language: Welcome, Mr. Karim. You have a room on the third floor.
sentences.target_language: أهلاً وسهلاً، السيد كريم. لديكم غرفة في الطابق الثالث.
sentences.romanized: ahlan wa-sahlan, as-sayyid Karim. ladaykum ghurfa fi at-tabiq ath-thalith.
sentences.literal: Welcome, the-mister Karim. You-have room on the-floor the-third.
sentences.grammarNote: لديكم (ladaykum) uses the plural 'your' suffix كم (kum) as a formal term of address — similar to using 'vous' in French. In Arabic, the formal plural is used to show respect to an individual guest. Compare لدي (I have) with لديكم (you — formal — have).

هَل (hal) is the yes/no question particle in Arabic — it turns any statement into a question. هَل فِي الغُرفَة...؟ = 'is there in the room...?' — the prepositional phrase فِي الغُرفَة acts as the predicate of the existential sentence. وَاي فَاي (wāy fāy) is a phonetic transcription of 'Wi-Fi' into Arabic letters.

sentences.base_language: Yes, of course. The password is written on the card.
sentences.target_language: نعم، بالطبع. كلمة المرور مكتوبة على البطاقة.
sentences.romanized: na'am, bit-tab'. kalimat al-murur maktuba 'ala al-bitaqa.
sentences.literal: Yes, by-the-nature. word the-passing written on the-card.
sentences.grammarNote: بِالطَّبع (bit-ṭabʿ) = of course, literally 'by nature.' كَلِمَة المُرُور (kalimat al-murūr) = password — iḍāfa: كَلِمَة (word) + المُرُور (the passing), from root م-ر-ر (to pass). مَكتُوبَة (maktūba, written, feminine) — passive participle مَفعُول from root ك-ت-ب — agrees with كَلِمَة (feminine).

sentences.base_language: At what time is breakfast served?
sentences.grammarNote: يُقَدَّم (yuqaddamu, is served/presented) — passive voice Form II: the double م indicates Form II, and the ضمة on يـ + فتحة on the last consonant marks the passive present. Passive voice in Arabic is formed by changing the vowels: يُقَدِّم (he presents, active) → يُقَدَّم (is presented, passive). الفُطُور (al-fuṭūr, breakfast) from root ف-ط-ر (to break fast).

sentences.base_language: Breakfast is from seven to ten in the morning.
sentences.target_language: الفطور من الساعة السابعة حتى العاشرة صباحاً.
sentences.romanized: al-futur min as-sa'a as-sabi'a hatta al-'ashira sabahan.
sentences.literal: The-breakfast from the-hour the-seventh until the-tenth morning.
sentences.grammarNote: الساعة السابعة (as-sāʿa as-sābiʿa) = seven o'clock, literally 'the seventh hour.' Arabic uses feminine ordinals to tell time: السابعة (seventh), العاشرة (tenth). مِن... حتى... (min... ḥattā...) = from... until/to... — a common time-range construction. صَبَاحاً (ṣabāḥan, in the morning) — indefinite accusative adverb of time.

sentences.base_language: Is there a gym in the hotel?
sentences.target_language: هل يوجد صالة رياضية في الفندق؟
sentences.romanized: hal yujad sala riyadata fi al-funduq?
sentences.literal: Q exists hall sports in the-hotel?
sentences.grammarNote: يُوجَد (yūjad, there is/exists) — passive Form IV of root و-ج-د (w-j-d, to find/exist). Literally 'is found.' A very common existential construction in MSA: هَل يُوجَد...؟ = is there a...? More colloquial: فِيه... (fīh...) or عِندَهُم... صَالَة رِيَاضِيَّة (ṣāla riyāḍiyya, sports hall/gym) — indefinite adjective phrase.

sentences.base_language: Yes, it is open until midnight.
sentences.grammarNote: تَفتَح (taftaḥ, it opens — feminine verb) refers back to الصالة (the hall, feminine). Arabic verbs agree with their subjects in gender: masculine يَفتَح (yaftaḥ), feminine تَفتَح (taftaḥ). مُنتَصَف اللَّيل (muntaṣaf al-layl, midnight) — iḍāfa: مُنتَصَف (middle, Form VIII passive participle of ن-ص-ف) + الليل (night).

sentences.base_language: The bellhop took the luggage and showed the guest to the room.
sentences.target_language: حمل حامل الأمتعة الحقائب وأرشد الضيف إلى الغرفة.
sentences.romanized: hamala hamil al-amti'a al-haqa'ib wa-arshada ad-dayf ila al-ghurfa.
sentences.literal: Carried carrier the-luggage the-bags and-guided the-guest to the-room.
sentences.grammarNote: حَامِل الأَمتِعَة (ḥāmil al-amtiʿa) = baggage carrier/bellhop — an iḍāfa: حَامِل (carrier, فَاعِل from ح-م-ل, to carry) + الأَمتِعَة (the luggage, broken plural of مَتَاع, goods). أَرشَدَ (arshada, to guide/show — Form IV) from root ر-ش-د (to guide correctly): رُشد (good judgment), مُرشِد (guide).

sentences.base_language: If you need anything, please call the front desk.
sentences.target_language: إذا كنت تحتاج أي شيء، من فضلك اتصل بمكتب الاستقبال.
sentences.romanized: idha kunta tahtaj ayy shay', min fadlak ittasil bi-maktab al-istiqbal.
sentences.literal: If you-were you-need any thing, from your-favor call to-desk the-reception.
sentences.grammarNote: إِذَا كُنتَ تَحتَاج (idhā kunta taḥtāj) = if you need — a conditional with the past tense كُنتَ after إِذَا, even though the meaning is present/future. This is the standard MSA conditional pattern: إِذَا + past verb in the condition, then the result clause follows. اِتَّصَلَ بـ (ittaṣala bi-, to call/contact) — Form VIII of و-ص-ل (to arrive/connect): اِتَّصَلَ bi- = to get in contact with.

sentences.base_language: The guest thanked him and settled into the comfortable room.
sentences.target_language: شكره الضيف واستقر في الغرفة المريحة.
sentences.romanized: shakkarahu ad-dayf wa-istaqarra fi al-ghurfa al-mariha.
sentences.literal: Thanked-him the-guest and-settled in the-room the-comfortable.
sentences.grammarNote: شَكَرَهُ الضَّيف (shakarahū ad-ḍayf) = the guest thanked him — the object pronoun ـهُ (him) is attached to the verb, and the subject الضيف follows. اِستَقَرَّ (istaqarra, to settle/become stable) — Form X of root ق-ر-ر (q-r-r, to be settled). اِستَقَرَّ فِي = to settle in/at. الغُرفَة المُرِيحَة — definite noun + definite adjective with الـ.
